findings.title: Handler function makes multiple repository calls
findings.issue: Handler function 'ListArticlesHandler' calls 18 repository methods directly. Handlers should delegate business logic to a service layer; the service layer is responsible for orchestrating multiple repository calls. Keeping handlers thin prevents logic duplication, improves testability, and maintains clean layer separation.

findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a service / use-case layer (e.g. UserService.CreateWithProfile) that encapsulates the multi-repository orchestration. The handler calls exactly one service method; the service method calls as many repositories as needed, managing transactions internally.

findings.title: Multiple database calls in a single repository function
findings.issue: Repository function 'GetArticlesAboveSevenDays' contains 2 database calls. Each repository function must execute exactly one database operation. Multiple DB calls can cause hidden N+1 problems, make transactions unclear, and break the single-responsibility principle.
offending_lines: line 2347: res, err := dblib.SelectRows(ctx, ar.Db, query, pgx.RowToStructByNameLax[domain.ArticleReceivedStatus])
line 2367: res1, err1 := dblib.SelectRows(ctx, ar.Db, query1, pgx.RowToStructByNameLax[domain.ArticleReceivedStatus])
findings.suggested_fix: Split this function into multiple single-responsibility repository functions, one per DB operation. If the operations are logically atomic, wrap them in a single database transaction and issue the calls through the transaction handle inside a dedicated helper.

findings.title: Database call inside a loop (N+1 query pattern)
findings.issue: Repository function 'InsertDeliveryAccountingDetails' executes a database call inside a for/range loop. This is the classic N+1 query anti-pattern: for N iterations the code issues N separate round-trips to the database, causing quadratic latency growth and connection-pool exhaustion.
findings.suggested_fix: Collect all required identifiers first, then issue a single bulk query using IN ($1, $2, ...) or pgx SendBatch. Alternatively, use a JOIN or a CTE to retrieve all related data in one round-trip.
